$schema_note: Golden vectors for pulldown-cmark's `$`/`$$` open-close rules. This is a CROSS-LANGUAGE CONTRACT: the Rust parser is checked against it by tests/math_delimiters.rs today, and the future CM6 editor grammar (design H20) must agree with the same file so the editor's highlighting and the build's parsing cannot drift. Every `expect_math_spans` value below was DERIVED BY RUNNING pulldown-cmark 0.13.3 with `parser_options(true)`, never by reasoning about the spec.
rules_as_measured: An opening `$` must NOT be followed by whitespace. `$ x $` is not math.

A closing `$` must be IMMEDIATELY preceded by a non-whitespace byte. This is the whole story behind the currency cases: `$5 and $10` is safe ONLY because the second `$` follows a space. Put any punctuation between two prices and it closes — `$5-$10`, `$5/$10`, `$9.99 ($8.99…)` are all math. The safety is a habit of English typography, not a property of ASCII or of currency.

The test is on bytes, and it is not ASCII-aware: a CJK comma is non-whitespace, so `一个$5，两个$10` closes and becomes math. This is the real-world false-positive risk for moss's Chinese-language authors.
Code spans and fenced code are lexed before math and never yield math events.
A backslash-escaped `\$` never opens or closes.

vectors.note: The base case: opener not followed by whitespace, closer preceded by non-whitespace.

The P1 regression case. With ENABLE_MATH and no handler arms this rendered as `

Energy .

` — the equation silently deleted.
Two independent spans; the prose between them is not swallowed.
Leading space after the opener disqualifies it. Not math.

SURPRISE vs the design doc, which listed ASCII currency as a false positive. It is NOT: the closing `$` is preceded by a space, so it cannot close. English-prose currency is safe by accident of spacing, which is exactly why the CJK case below is dangerous — the safety is not a rule, it is a habit of English typography.

A price RANGE is not safe, unlike `$5 and $10`: the closing `$` is preceded by `-`, a non-whitespace byte, so it closes. The counterexample to 'English currency is safe' — the hyphen, not the language, is what decides.
A parenthetical price: the closing `$` is preceded by `(`. Same rule, and the span swallows the opening parenthesis.
Slash-separated prices close for the same reason. Any punctuation between two prices reopens this whole class.
Same reason: every `$` here follows a space. Realistic English prose with three prices survives untouched.

THE false positive that matters for moss. CJK text has no inter-word spaces, so the second `$` is preceded by `，` (non-whitespace) and closes. `5，两个` becomes an equation. This is why `[site].math` is a per-site switch and why `moss doctor --math` scans imports.
False positive in ASCII too, once punctuation rather than a space precedes the closer: `(` before `$10` is non-whitespace.
Backslash-escaped dollars never participate. This is the author's escape hatch when prose genuinely trips the rule.
Code spans are lexed first. Math never fires inside one.
Fenced code likewise. This is the empirical basis for defaulting `[site].math` on: every `$` found across the sibling repos lives inside fenced code.
Display math. Note the TeX retains its surrounding spaces verbatim — `$$` does not trim, unlike the inline opener's whitespace rule.
Multi-line display math keeps its newlines in the TeX payload.
The TeX payload is RAW — pulldown does no HTML escaping. Escaping is moss's job at AST construction, since the node is a raw-passthrough `Inline::Other`.
